messages.content: What three counts set a cell's state?

degree (how many edges read it -- circulation), population (how many cells share its shape -- pressure), and churn (rate of new or varied members -- temperature). High churn + low degree tends to gas; low churn + high degree tends to ice; water is the circulating middle. They read the same for a Blueprint, a Recipe, or a NamedCell.

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn oe-plan (rows)
        (if (eq (len rows) 0) (empty)
            (if (eq (oe-needs? (oc-state (head rows))) 1)
                (cons (head rows) (oe-plan (tail rows)))
                (oe-plan (tail rows)))))

oe-plan is a Form stdlib recipe taking 1 argument(s): rows. Structurally, it is recursive (it calls itself), and it branches with if, and it builds a list with cons. Authored intent: the PLAN: the sublist of rows that still need install (catalog order preserved)
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: b #off  (off in instructions) : base 0x14000000 | off
(defn fa-b    (off)       (fa-asm 335544320 (list 1) (list off)))
Explain why this Form guard is buggy: (if (empty xs) A B)

It is buggy because empty constructs the absence value rather than testing for it: (empty xs) returns the empty list [], which if treats as truthy, so the branch is always A. The fix is (if (eq (len xs) 0) A B).
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: read the command from fk_src: input_byte i -> the i-th byte, stop at NUL/newline.

(defn fc-read (i acc)
        (do (let b (input_byte i))
            (if (eq b 0) acc
                (if (eq b 10) acc
                    (fc-read (add i 1) (str_concat acc (byte_to_str b)))))))
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: whisper's two-conv stem: conv1 (k3 s1 p1) → gelu → conv2 (k3 s2 p1) → gelu

(defn cv-stem (input W1 b1 W2 b2)
  (cv-conv-gelu W2 b2 (cv-conv-gelu W1 b1 input 1 1 3) 1 2 3))
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: the dormant DNA the restoration re-grounds: the cell's own composition.
(defn sh-dna (cell) (node_children cell))

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn gm-call-suffix (g exprp c caps)
        (do (let cc (skip-ws c))
            (if (eq (cur-peek cc) 60)
                (do (let gc (gen-scan (cur-advance cc 1) 1))
                    (if (nil? gc) (empty)
                        (do (let cg2 (skip-ws gc))
                            (if (eq (cur-peek cg2) 40) (gm-args g exprp (cur-advance cg2 1) caps) (empty)))))
            (if (eq (cur-peek cc) 40)
                (gm-args g exprp (cur-advance cc 1) caps)
                (empty)))))

gm-call-suffix is a Form stdlib recipe taking 4 argument(s): g, exprp, c, caps. Structurally, it branches with if. Authored intent: call suffix: optional <typelist> then a required ( args ) → (argnodes cur) | (empty)

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn lenses () (list "meaning" "feeling" "trust" "sovereignty" "vitality"))
lenses is a Form stdlib recipe taking no arguments. Authored intent: the five lenses "what IS" projects onto
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: the oracle predictor = mean target over the training corpus

(defn acf-mean-y (corpus)
    (div (acf-sum-y corpus) (len corpus)))
What is the fourth arm (fkwu) and why must every band touch it?

fkwu is the emitted universal kernel -- the fourth proof arm alongside Go, Rust, and TypeScript. Form/BML runtime proof walks all four; a band that never touched fkwu is not proven. The fourth is a kernel, not a footnote. fkwu has two faces from one emit: a proof-walker giving four-way agreement, and a self-JIT that crystallizes hot pure functions to native and melts them on cool.
What is the proof floor for a new Form band?

Four kernels: Go, Rust, TypeScript, and the emitted universal walker fkwu. validate.sh always runs Go/Rust/TS; when the band's stem is listed in form/fourth-arm-bands.txt it also runs fkwu and prints 'fourth arm: ... four-way'. Success is the intended verdict with '1 ok, 0 divergent'. A band that never touched the fourth arm is not proven.

Write a Form stdlib recipe for: a mel row's energy: slaney weights · bin powers over the row's support (bins + weights are DATA from the filterbank cell, exactly as spectrum.fk carries its band coefficients)

(defn mf-mel-energy (xs ks ws N)
  (if (eq (len ks) 0) 0.0
      (add (mul (head ws) (mf-bin-power xs (head ks) N))
           (mf-mel-energy xs (tail ks) (tail ws) N))))
How does form-cli ask reach the local oracle?

The ask verb renders an ollama /api/generate request body in Form, POSTs it via http-fetch to http://localhost:11434/api/generate, then parses the JSON reply in Form to lift the 'response' field. The whole ask is flow, choice, and string ops on the emitted kernel -- no host-exec, no subprocess, no clang/go at run time.
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: page count = ceil(codeLimit / 4096); a full multiple stays exact.
(defn co-npages (n) (if (le n 4096) 1 (add 1 (co-npages (sub n 4096)))))
What is the role of JIT in the host kernel?

JIT is one running composite the kernel builds and trusts: it crystallizes a hot recipe into a native plugin (recipe -> native code, cached by NodeID) and melts it on cool, with hysteresis. It is an example of the kernel choosing and A/B testing implementations of the core spec over host resources -- the open-primitive-set mechanism that installs a hot composite back into the named primitive set as a callable leaf.

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn tr-validated-rate (o min-samples)
  (if (ge (tr-o-attempts o) min-samples) (tr-o-rate o) 0))

tr-validated-rate is a Form stdlib recipe taking 2 argument(s): o, min-samples. Structurally, it branches with if. Authored intent: VALIDATED rate: the held-out rate ONLY if attempts >= min-samples, else 0 (unproven). A capability with too few heldout trials is unvalidated, not sovereign.

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn file-get (store key) (cls-get store key))
file-get is a Form stdlib recipe taking 2 argument(s): store, key. Authored intent: get is already the 0-or-1 optional the port expects.
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: angular difference on the circle (deg), 0..180
(defn abs-deg (d) (if (lt d 0.0) (mul -1.0 d) d))
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: cross-attention using the precomputed ck/cv (no enc re-projection)

(defn tb-mh-cross-sub-cached (dlns ck cv wq bq wo bo n hd scale)
  (tb-proj-seq wo bo (tb-mh-attn (tb-proj-seq wq bq dlns) ck cv n hd scale)))
What is the role of JIT in the host kernel?

JIT is one running composite the kernel builds and trusts: it crystallizes a hot recipe into a native plugin (recipe -> native code, cached by NodeID) and melts it on cool, with hysteresis. It is an example of the kernel choosing and A/B testing implementations of the core spec over host resources -- the open-primitive-set mechanism that installs a hot composite back into the named primitive set as a callable leaf.
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: the governing teaching, queryable in the substrate.
(defn gematria-channel-teaching () "lc-gematria-as-content-addressing")
In Form, does eq compare strings?
No. eq compares integers and nodes; str_eq compares strings. Don't cross them -- use eq for numeric/node equality and str_eq for string equality.
Name the four phase transitions and what each does.

CONDENSE (gas -> water): a diffuse cell whose circulation crosses condense-min settles into flow. FREEZE (water -> ice): a fluid, stable, widely-read cell crystallizes. MELT (ice -> water): a frozen cell, still referenced but now changing, returns to flow. SUBLIMATE (ice -> gas): a frozen cell whose circulation falls to ~0 releases its hold and returns to potential -- removing nothing; NodeID and kind persist.

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn sc-cell-value (blueprint ctor)
        (str_concat (sc-key blueprint) (str_concat " " (sc-key ctor))))
sc-cell-value is a Form stdlib recipe taking 2 argument(s): blueprint, ctor. Structurally, it works with strings. Authored intent: value encodes "blueprint-key ctor-key"

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn mv-native-http-pulse-sample (source url response floor north)
        (do
            (let body (mv-http-response-body response))
            (let root (parse-json url body))
            (mv-production-pulse-sample
                source
                url
                (json-string-value (json-object-get root "as_of"))
                (json-string-value (json-object-get root "overall"))
                (json-int-value (json-object-get root "silences"))
                (mv-http-response-duration-ms response)
                (str_len body)
                floor
                north)))

mv-native-http-pulse-sample is a Form stdlib recipe taking 5 argument(s): source, url, response, floor, north. Structurally, it works with strings. Authored intent: Requires json.fk when called; JSON body parsing stays in Form instead of relying on sibling-specific convenience natives.

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn cell-blueprint (c) (ps-nth (node_children c) 2))
cell-blueprint is a Form stdlib recipe taking 1 argument(s): c. Authored intent: cell-blueprint -> the NodeID, the structural pointer, returned as-is.

Explain this Form recipe:
(defn ag-ffn-layer (seed)
        (list "ffn"
              (list (list (add 0.2 seed) -0.1) (list 0.1 (add 0.3 seed))) (list 0.0 0.0)
              (list (list 0.4 0.1) (list -0.2 0.5)) (list 0.0 0.0)))
ag-ffn-layer is a Form stdlib recipe taking 1 argument(s): seed. Authored intent: a deterministic FFN-residual layer, seeded by its depth index (distinct, reproducible weights).
Write a Form stdlib recipe for: (diff-children child-diffs) → DIFF-CHILDREN recipe. Children are the per-position child diffs, one per child of the original pair.

(defn diff-children (child-diffs)
    (intern_node DIFF-CHILDREN child-diffs))

Write a Form stdlib recipe for: make-cell-recipe name domain blueprint ctor -> CELL Recipe. name and domain are strings; blueprint is a NodeID passed through as child 2; ctor is a composed Recipe NodeID as child 3. Content- addressed: the same four arguments always intern to the same CELL NodeID on every sibling kernel, and that is the dedup.

(defn make-cell-recipe (name domain blueprint ctor)
    (intern_node CELL-V0
        (list (intern_trivial_string name)
              (intern_trivial_string domain)
              blueprint
              ctor)))
